item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent. Glad you enjoyed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I absolutely love how menacing it all feels, the ending really packed the ''OH SH*T'' kind of vibe. So an easy +1 from me.

item.title: End of Contest
item.content:encoded:
Alright, at +150, we hit 15th place, which is far better than I've done in any of the X000 contests so far. I even managed to grab my first choice slot.

Thank you everyone who upvoted this during the contest.

Allow me to introduce SCP-5060.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks M_E, glad you enjoyed it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a sucker for a good extraterrestrial skip and this delivered with aplomb. Also, brilliant title. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this may be the shortest entry I've read so far. that's not a bad thing; at this point, it's an advantage, at least for me.

Gotta cash in on the reader fatigue from all those 10K+ word entries.

In all seriousness, I'm glad you enjoyed the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this may be the shortest entry I've read so far. that's not a bad thing; at this point, it's an advantage, at least for me. short, simple, solid concept, and very mysterious… and surprisingly scary with that bit at the end. I sure hope the foundation, who rarely seem to know when to leave well enough alone, stops trying to probe these guys. they seem willing to leave us alone if we quit fucking with them. maybe 2935 was a warning (not the first time I've seen it crosslinked in a 5k entry). and the north sentinel island vibes are a bonus. I dig it a lot. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it's short, sweet, and intriguing.

Just what I was going for. Glad you liked the piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sometimes you just need to be blunt to get the point across. :V

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really like this one! Especially since North Sentinel island has a special place in my heart. I think the mystery aspect is a lot more understated than other entries, but that doesn't do much to hurt it in my opinion, I think it's short, sweet, and intriguing. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, this explains a whole lot! :D

Mostly, I just like the idea of a planet noticing we're probing them and then telling us exactly what they think of that in the strongest possible terms.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad you enjoyed it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The reason you gave as to why the Foundation is getting involved with this planet is that it could potentially be anomalous. As I see it, the Foundation wouldn't list something as an SCP object until it has proven it's anomalous properties. For example, imagine if SCP-173 never moved around. Someone may suggest that it possesses the ability to move, but it hasn't demonstrated this anomalous property in any capacity. If, as you say, the inhabitants of 5000 are "reality benders", I'd like to see that shown in the article. To me, this would prove it's anomalous properties and warrant Foundation intervention.

I mean, the other reason why the Foundation would be poking around here is that is the whole point of Project Heimdall: "Research potential means by which extraterrestrial intelligence displaying vigorous, active hostility to human life and/or civilization might attempt to conquer, enslave, or exterminate humanity. Analyze likely outcomes. Develop countermeasures and contingency plans."

As for your other points, I really don't have an interest in expanding on this article further. I feel the brevity works for the narrative I'm telling, and adding those additional paragraphs and details will bog the writing down. If the article as is doesn't do enough for you, that's fine, I'll just take that loss for what it is.

For the record though, I do appreciate your feedback greatly. I do, however, feel we are not seeing each other eye to eye as to what the article is meant to be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for taking a look at my feedback! In case I didn't say this before, I do enjoy your SCP object. All I wanted to express in my post is that I'd like to see you flesh this out a little more. I want to make a few comments on your response as well as clarify a few things.

Firstly, one of the fundamental things I have learned about astrobiology is that we can't make assumptions about what form extraterrestrial life will take. For all we know, the life on this planet may very well be capable of launching these city-sized satellites into space with their bear hands. Obviously this is just conjecture (and I'd think something capable of that would be pretty anomalous), so take this with a grain of salt.

The reason you gave as to why the Foundation is getting involved with this planet is that it could potentially be anomalous. As I see it, the Foundation wouldn't list something as an SCP object until it has proven it's anomalous properties. For example, imagine if SCP-173 never moved around. Someone may suggest that it possesses the ability to move, but it hasn't demonstrated this anomalous property in any capacity. If, as you say, the inhabitants of 5000 are "reality benders", I'd like to see that shown in the article. To me, this would prove it's anomalous properties and warrant Foundation intervention.

Lastly, I'd like to ask for clarification on something. For one, in reading the article, I didn't come under the impression that the civilization had instantaneously moved to Venus, as you put it. Rather I thought they had been there prior to the Foundation's investigations on their home planet, maybe using it as a forward operating base (not sure if I'm using this term correctly, sorry if I didn't), perhaps for the purpose of observing us. Like with the other thing I said about demonstrating anomalous ability, I'd like to see this civilization demonstrate their anomalous abilities in this regard as well.

Once again, thank you for taking the time to reply to my post and read through this one as well. I appreciate you considering my feedback, even if it's only to refute my arguments and disprove them. When I say this isn't SCP-5000-worthy material, I don't mean that is shouldn't be an SCP. I definitely think that, with some more work, it would make a great one. Thanks again for considering what I have to say!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad you enjoyed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

this planet seems to simply be a venus-like planet that happens to house extraterrestrial life

Given how hostile the conditions on Venus are to what we would traditionally consider terrestrial life, saying something is "simply venus like planet that houses extraterrestrial life" is a bit of missing the forest for the trees.

is on or near a level close to us in terms of technology.

But the thing is, the Foundation doesn't know that. They have absolutely zero ideas of the capabilities of the inhabitants of 5000 here. For all they know its a race of reality benders, or it could be little more than a group of primitive civilizations with extra strong catapults. They don't know and are determined to find out.

Their size is remarkably large for something to launch into space. However, should you consider the lower gravity (due to it's smaller size) and its atmospheric differences, it's possible that this civilization would be able to produce the means needed to launch these things into space.

Possibly, but since this group has the ability to launch items larger than most cities into space on a fairly frequent basis, an ability we as humans lack, I see no reason for the Foundation to not consider that feat anomalous until proven otherwise (which is difficult given their inability to observe the civilization).

it's not hard to imagine they could transport a lot of it to Venus and fire some slugs at specific locations from there.

Perhaps, but given that this kind of exodus occurred nearly instantaneously, and now Venus appears to have the anomalous properties of SCP-5000, its not so easy to just say they up and moved via traditional transportation means. And, there is nothing to suggest they simply have better rocket fuel.

This is my weakest argument of the bunch, but this may just have been something they came up with on their own, with the strange script being their language or something.

Given how specific the imagery is on the Voyager disc, it's incredibly unlikely they just so happened to create an identical replica of it on their own.

this may not be a very anomalous object

Given the above, I have to disagree with you.

at the very least isn't SCP-5000 worthy.

Don't see why this would be the case. It's an article that presents a mystery. This is a mystery contest. There is nothing here disqualifying this article from the SCP-5000 slot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After reading through this, I find myself thinking that this may not be a very anomalous object or, at the very least isn't SCP-5000 worthy. Let me list out each part and my personal explanation for it:

- The planet itself: Compared to the exoplanets we have observed throughout our own galaxy, such as GJ 504b, the pink Jupiter, HD189733b, the fun glass rain planet, and even Neptune and Uranus, where it rains diamonds, this planet seems to simply be a venus-like planet that happens to house extraterrestrial life that is on or near a level close to us in terms of technology. The only anomalous pieces about this planet are their ability to see us when we cannot see them, as far as I can tell.
- The satellites: These appear to be mass graves or graveyards. Their size is remarkably large for something to launch into space. However, should you consider the lower gravity (due to it's smaller size) and its atmospheric differences, it's possible that this civilization would be able to produce the means needed to launch these things into space.
- The laser: This, if we go along with my theory regarding the satellites, is very easily explained. If you can get a 50km radius structure up to escape velocity, it shouldn't be too difficult to get a small 20kg projectile up to super fast speeds.
- The "meteor shower": This is slightly difficult to explain, but not really. Continuing with the theory of their rocket fuel being better than ours, it's not hard to imagine they could transport a lot of it to Venus and fire some slugs at specific locations from there.
- The Voyager Disc: This is my weakest argument of the bunch, but this may just have been something they came up with on their own, with the strange script being their language or something.

These are kind of just spitball ideas to see if I can explain away this. I've got a bit of a background in astronomy so I thought I'd have some fun. Anyways, I'd love to see you deepen this and explore deeper into the mystery of this planet. Let me know what you think!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What I like about the mystery of the disk acquisition is that it implies that the aliens are either
A: Wide spread across the galaxy, or B: Doing something very weird and anomalous, such as stalking humanity. Both are scary.

This is a very nice and concise mystery. I think there are stronger 5k contestants, but this one is really charming in its simpleness and effectiveness. Also I love space SCPs, so +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this entry, to me, the unknown of space is always interesting and this entry was a great shorter read than a long read than can't keep my interest for long. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Even though it’s nothing grand in my eyes, I think it easily deserves a place as one of the first Series VI SCPs.

Glad you enjoyed the piece. I will admit that this is not as lofty or as far-reaching as other entries. That said, you don't need to reinvent the wheel to tell a good mystery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this sums it up very well. The ending had great potential but the buildup needed more detail. Perhaps starting with a longer and more mysterious origin (maybe the disappearance of a voyager probe?)

I feel setting that up would bog down the article more than it would be worth. Additionally, how the inhabitants of the planet acquired the disk is part of the mystery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this sums it up very well. The ending had great potential but the buildup needed more detail. Perhaps starting with a longer and more mysterious origin (maybe the disappearance of a voyager probe?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's a bigger grave yard than an entire alternate dimension?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. You want mystery, you've got it. Also, a 2935 reference is kinda terrifying.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad you enjoyed it, Zyn!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Foundation: These aliens don't seem to care for us messing with them all to much.

Also the Foundation: Probe them again!

In any case, glad you enjoyed the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, they were able to procure the probes we deemed irretrievable, ascertain the locations of some our most prominent grave sites and bombard them accordingly in a return-to-sender fashion, then top it off with the most brazen illustration of human extinction on our own record (just in case we didn’t get the message). This hits me as the most hilariously potent display of power, which makes me want to leave them the hell alone, but also continue being nosy because I need to know more.

All accomplished in a relatively short article. I love it.
Plus one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is why I like this entry. Was a good read during the drafting stage; I'm glad to see it posted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was a fine line to walk between brevity and substance, but I feel like I got the sweet spot nailed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You got it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, considering the descriptions of the satellites around the planet, and whats contained within(spheres with trace element dust), that they are basically space cemeteries holding the ashes of thousands of the planets inhabitants each, and the subsequent launch of the Foundations own probes at large grave sites and cemeteries on earth is a form of retaliation?

Overall, pretty neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Short, sweet, and to the point. I feel that other skips in this contest might bog down the mystery in a plethora of logs, but you manage to be unsettling and make the space angle work while leaving a lot of questions wide fucking open. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks. I'm quite pleased with the title as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the contents and title match very well.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad you enjoyed it! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My favorite SCPs - not just in this contest but in general, going all the way back to SCP-093 my first and forever favorite - leave me wanting to know more. This really scratches that itch. I desperately want to know what this species is and how they can Naruto themselves into our solar system like that. The threat posed here is so tantalizing. I adore it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for your review!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+Opinions
– hide block

I dunno. Usually I'm a sucker for space-based SCPs, and the crosslink to 2935 (the implication that the aliens know about O, Death is scary in its own right) as well as the clever way through which the aliens communicated their intent (and the unsettling fact that they somehow have access to the Voyager records) did pique my interest, but overall, I feel like this article didn't leave much of an impression on me. The "less is more" approach clearly seems to have worked for a majority of this article's readers (and has worked for me a lot of times in the past as well), so maybe this is just down to individual preference and what I personally like getting out of shorter articles like these; whatever the case, it's a novote from me for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love it, but I don't think an interplanetary conflict about misconduct on graveyards is necessarily 5000 material.

‾\_(ツ)_/‾

I don't see why that would be the case. Its an article with a mystery in a mystery contest. You do you, my dude, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this 5k is out of this world!

Ayyyyyyy

Thank you for the kind words, UN

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love it, but I don't think an interplanetary conflict about misconduct on graveyards is necessarily 5000 material.
Novote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this 5k is out of this world! jokes aside, this piece has a very strong and immediately engaging hook for a story that keeps on giving, which is ironic as this article took the 'less is more' approach. a very good, fun, and short read. not a whole lot more to say other than good job, conwell!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is indeed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this perhaps a reference to North Sentinel island? +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you.

I'm glad to see that the "less is more" approach paid off here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I made it clear how much I enjoyed this in the draft stages, and the minor changes that have happened since then only improve the piece. A solid upvote, and a nice breath of fresh air amongst some of the longer pieces.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I’ve been a sucker for SCPs in outer space as of late, so seeing a 5kon entry that not only is one of these but manages to tell just enough with so little information earns this article a +1 from me. Even though it’s nothing grand in my eyes, I think it easily deserves a place as one of the first Series VI SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 Well composed and in space. I love space SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This did just enough for me - any more and I might have been turned off, but this stopped at exactly the point where I was most intrigued. Well-judged.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the quick reply. I’m a sucker for space-related SCPs so you’ve earned my +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's well composed and has an interesting premise, but I don't feel that much from it. The ending in particular had that intrigue you expect from skips, but it just wasn't built up enough to be satisfying for me.

Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Edit made.

So does the final part about Venus imply that this species is widespread?

It implies that they have the means and ability to get to Venus undetected.

Also I might be missing the information but how far away is this exoplanet?

I intentionally left this vague, but suffice to say it's close enough for the Foundation to have observed it before the use of FTL probes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As I said when I read the draft: the more I thought about this one, the more I liked it. It isn't long, but it packs a compelling premise and good sense of threat into a small space. Plus, it reminds me of Fiasco (in a good way).

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So does the final part about Venus imply that this species is widespread?

Also I might be missing the information but how far away is this exoplanet?

Finally there’s a couple tone issues I spotted, notably the “is” in this sentence:
“Additionally, the 28th projectile contained an iron copy of the Voyager Golden Record in which all imagery of humans is scratched out.”

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Well, here we are. Another X000 Contest on our doorstep, and another attempt to swing for the fences. As anyone who read Portland Vice could probably tell you, mystery is not my strong suit. So here I wanted to come up with something that got in, presented a mystery, and got out without too much time to bog itself down with chaff and other distractions. There will be plenty of novella-length entries posted, so I wanted to keep this one brief.

Special thanks to all the following for their help:

+ show block
– hide block
  • A Random Day x2
  • AlanDaris
  • Calibri Bold
  • Captain Kirby
  • ch00bakka x2
  • DarkStuff x2
  • DrAkimoto
  • DrBleep x2
  • DrMagnus
  • Elogee FishTruck
  • gee0765
  • Jekeled
  • LilyFlower
  • MaliceAforethought
  • ManyMeats
  • N_Aepic_Fael
  • NatVoltaic
  • RockTeethMothEyes
  • stormbreath
  • stormfallen
  • TheMightyMcB
  • T Rutherford
  • Tuomey Tombstone
  • TyGently
  • Uncle Nicolini
  • UraniumEmpire
  • Zyn

My runner up spots include:

  • SCP-5060
  • SCP-5760
  • SCP-5560
  • SCP-5960
  • SCP-5360

Image source: https://www.flickr.com/photos/kevinmgill/24771639777

Thank you for the read, and for your time.

JC
